sentences.text: Well, what happens, I, I made my way to the train.
And I got on the train with some difficulty and explanations in getting through the, the, the police inspections that were a few times during the train-- during the traveling of these three hours.
I got to the-- to the town of Sieldce.
I knew-- I had an address.
I didn't know exactly where they were.
But according to the address where my sister was, I found the place.
It-- that was 1941, winter.
It was kind of, of a ghetto, a-- not in comparison to Warsaw at all.
It was barbed wire mostly.
Compared to Warsaw, it looked to me like a-- like a nice paradise.
Because coming out of there, nothing was really as bad as Warsaw.
So I got myself acquainted very quick with the situation.
My aunt, at that time-- not remarried, she was an old maid.
So she married a gentleman with two children, which I met him.
Just before the war, she was married.
I knew them.
I met him one time.
And I was happy to get there.
I was happy to see my sister.
[CRYING] And life started over there.
My parents were still in Warsaw.
And I think within about two weeks' time, my father came.
My father came.
He was really exhausted.
I remember he came-- he walked.
He was not able to come with the railroad because he-- they would recognize him immediately that he was a Jew.
He walked all the way this-- whatever 100 kilometers distance was.
He walked, and he made his way.
And we, of course, were happy to see him.
Following my father, my mother came.
She came by railroad.
She played a deaf mute.
She did not speak, but she looked Polish.
And somehow, she was able to, to get by.
So we were together again.
And we stayed with my aunt in her apartment.
She had a two-room apartment, I remember.
So they gave us one room.
And we stayed in that room.
And I had a cousin.
I don't remember-- there was a girl also.
But somehow, I don't remember the girl very well.
But I remember well my cousin.
My cousin was about my age-- my new cousin.
And I tried to make friends with him.
But he was kind of-- not my kind of a guy.
He was slow and not very sharp.
And to me, he was-- he was, like, coming from another world.
I, I suppose I learned quick the, the way of life.
I grew up maybe faster than him.
So things started to go again in the ghetto.
You had to go out.
You had to make some way, bring some food in.
Because it was still a ghetto.
It wasn't a, a, you know, a ghetto like in Warsaw, but there were barbed wire.
There were-- actually, there was basically one gate to the whole ghetto that I remember.
It was a main street running through.
And the ghetto was-- the gate was facing the, the, the main street.
And I used to make my way out through the barbed wire.
There was-- on every street, next to the barbed wire was a patrol walking, a German with a rifle.
And if anybody tried to get out through the barbed wire, he would get shot.
So I would wait with my father over there.

And the moment the German would pass that point and continue walking with his back toward me, my father would spread the wire, and I'd make my way through-- make my way through, hit another street, and get away.
So I was never caught.
Somehow, the German never turned around to see me.
And coming back, I could come through the gate.
They would not stop me from coming through the gate.
Whatever I carried, it was OK.
It, it wasn't really tight over there.
But they would not let you out.
There was no way, if you did not have any papers, to go out.
I remember my father, in the beginning, he had a paper to work on the Aryan side.
He was able to do some work in a-- in a German hospital as a plumber.
He had many friends in that town because he grew up over there.
So for a while, he was able to, to work, which I used to go out of the ghetto and meet him over there.
So sometimes, he would go to the-- to the kitchen and get some food for me.
And we kind of arranged things.
So he had the papers to go out and come in.
But that ended also.
How long did you stay in Sieldce for?
Well, I stayed in Sieldce from this winter, 1941, until August of 1942, which was the end of the ghetto.
We, we lived in the ghetto by-- later on, in 1942-- by actually begging for food.
I remember my mother would go out from the ghetto.
My father would do the same thing for her what he did for me.
He would open the wire.
And she would go out.
And she would go on the outskirts and simply beg for food from house to house.
And they would give us some bread.
And she would come through the gate.
And she was able to come through also.
And she would go out a few times a week, not every day, but every once in a while.
I heard that she was begging for food.
And at one time, she came in.
And she-- they were talking about it over there.
She was not able to go anymore.
I don't know what happened.
She walked into a house, and-- if they tried to rape her or they raped her.
I had no idea, but it was very unpleasant.
And they did not let me listen to the story.
But I know from there on, my mother was not going out anymore.
So I was the only provider for food.
I remember, I used to go to-- I used to go out from the ghetto.
I had a little can, like, I don't know, maybe two quarts or something, a metal can container.
And there was a German-- a army camp that-- I would walk in over there.
And a lot of kids would wait for the kitchen.
When the soldiers would finish eating, whatever was leftover, they would give the kids food.
So I was able to get some food.
The poor-- the kids wouldn't know that I was Jewish.
I was one among them.
And just like they stayed over there in the kitchen in line to get some food, I was also getting my next thing.
And they would fill up, give what was left over.
I would get my can of food, and I'd come to the ghetto.
And everything was fine.
And that was going on, on, and on until almost to the last days of the finishing out of the ghetto.
OK.
Right.
So things were very quiet and very good at the-- by the end of this situation.
We almost thought that we might be able to live like that, who knows how long?
Very, very-- they were very lenient.
They weren't coming in and grabbing people to work that much.
It was kind of a period of very, very quiet, that they kind of left us alone.
It was, like, too good to be true.
And one morning, we heard shots firing.
And we, within, like, maybe a half an hour-- it was at dawn-- we saw, already, in the ghetto, we saw Ukrainian, Germans, and Polish police rounding up Jews.
They came, they pulled everybody out of the house, and they lined them up in the street.
And they were doing, like, systematically, house by house by house.
So they came to our house, and there was no way to hide.
We weren't prepared to hide in any place.
They pulled us all out.
I remember, my mother-- it was summer.
It was really hot, but she made me wear another suit.
She-- I had some, some kind of an old suit.
She made me put that suit on.
And I didn't want it, I was fighting her.
But she made me put these clothes on.
And they took us all out from the house and into the middle of the street.
And they were leading us to an old cemetery that wasn't used anymore as a cemetery.
But it was like a-- like a empty football field, a huge place.
I used to play over there football sometimes with the kids.
We didn't have a ball, but we used to-- from a rag, we used to make a ball and kick it around.
That was in the-- in the good times, before they finished off the ghetto.
So they took us all house by house, street by street.
They brought all the Jews, and they put them in-- into that thing.
Everybody had to sit on whatever.
There were no belongings because they didn't let us take any belongings.
I mean, you could take whatever you carried on you, like, maybe a little bundle, things like that.
So everybody was together.
My father, mother, and my sister, we were all together, sitting down in this little spot in, in this old cemetery.
The cemetery was surrounded.
From one side was the main street and the open gate.
On both sides were a, a, a wall about, might be, I don't know, 8-foot, 10-foot-high walls.
And on one side was, like, an apartment complex.
So in every window of this apartment complex was a Ukrainian sitting over there with a rifle.
And the walls were sitting, also-- Ukrainians on both side of the walls.
And in the front, there was, like, a big opening that they were bringing in more and more people.
And the SS would bring in the people in, like, the Polish policeman with the SS would bring in the people.
And every time they brought in a group of people, they made them sit down.
They made them sit down.
They were filling up the place completely like that.
And these Ukrainians from the windows started to take shots at people that were moving or getting up.
Everybody had to sit.
So that was, like, morning.
By the time they brought all the people in-- was probably, maybe, noontime or so.
Took about three, four hours to bring all the people into this big place.
And they kept on shooting all the time.
Whoever moved was shot.
And at one time, I remember somebody-- no, that was the second day already.
Anyway, after the first day, there were quite a few dead people around us.
So at night, they made us move to another edge of this field.
We moved to another edge, and the dead ones were laying more on one side.
Seems like there was some more space on the other side, so they moved us over.
And we spent the night over there.
And in the morning, they were taking out men.
And I remember they took my father.
And we never thought we're going to see him again.
But somehow, like, maybe an hour later, he came back.
He was all beaten up.
And he was telling us that they made, like a-- like a, a line.
There were Germans and Ukrainians on both side of the sidewalk.
And they had to walk through the sidewalk.
And everybody was hitting them with whatever they had, their rubber hoses or butts, I don't know.
Whoever made it, they let them back in.
But there were very few that made it.
My father was lucky, and he made it through that whole thing.
He came back.
He came back.
And that was the second day that we spent over there.
Well, we stayed there for three days in that place.
On the third day, in the morning, we were dehydrated, filthy, bloody, and all that.
They brought in the fire department from the main streets.
And they poured hoses over us with water.
But they opened the hoses.
And anybody getting up, they would shoot, you see?
But they knew that everybody was dehydrated, and everybody's going to get up and run for water.
So they, they just played a game with us, you know.
They poured the water on.
And whoever went up to get a-- to get-- to get wet or something was shot.
And some of the water sprayed over us.
But the, the, the hoses weren't actually reaching the people.
That was just a game.
So they did that game.
And they killed some more people.
And by noontime, there was a rumor that they're going to take everybody out, and they're going to take us to the railroad station.
So everybody got up.
Everybody lined up.
And we started to walk to the railroad station.
At that time, the coat-- I mean, the suit that my mother dressed me actually was so beneficial to me because she told me that I should take it off now.
So when I got off this dirty, wet suit from blood, I had a clean change of clothes underneath.
And this saved my life, really.
Because walking toward the railroad station, my father told me that I have to run away.
I have to make my way out of the situation.
I have to be the survivor.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] Of course, I didn't want to listen to that.
I didn't want to separate from my parents.
I said, I'm not going to do it.
I'm going to go.
And we knew that we're probably going to Treblinka because we heard rumors of Treblinka, and so on.
And nobody ever came from there to tell what happened there.
So we knew it was the end of us.
But there was no way that you could run, or go, or do anything.
If you made one move, you were shot right on the spot.
Walking-- you walked in the street, in the middle of the street.
And on the side of the sidewalk, there were Germans, Ukrainians, SS, Polish policemen walking on the sidewalk.
There were very few people standing, you know, stand by-- people-- I mean, onlookers from the Polish.
There were some groups of people by the-- by the houses standing on the side.
And we walked in the middle.
Anybody that made a move was shot right on the spot over there.
You had no way of getting away from it.
So everybody kept on walking toward the railroad.
Well, the thing-- I did not know what was going on.
I just kept on walking.
The street, the road, kind of turned, you know, like a-- like around bend or something.
And at that point, my father pushed me.
It took probably a split of a second.
But when he pushed me, I found myself standing among these onlookers from the house.
There were, like, maybe four or five people, Polacks, that stood there, watching us walking in the middle of the street.
And as he pushed me, there was-- right away, an SS man came, looking at everybody that stood there.
But he wouldn't recognize me.
Luckily, I didn't have these bloody clothes on.
He would probably recognize me immediately.
So I stood there.
The Polacks didn't say one word.
Everybody was in shock-- and myself, too.
I, I don't know.
He-- we just took a split of a second.
He went by.
Came another one-- a Polish policeman came.
They knew something happened.
But it was so quick.
He must have been looking for a situation, my father.
And this was the situation that he found.
And I found myself standing over there.
I stood there maybe for about, oh, maybe five minutes.
Nobody said a word.
All of the sudden, one of the Polacks says, well, we better disperse, otherwise they will think that we are one of the Jews, like giving me to understand that it's time to move on.
And when I heard that, I started to move.
Well, I wanted to run-- see what happened to my parents.
So I continued going with the sidewalk, which were very few Polacks walking.
But against the wall, I made my way through-- forward to see what happened ahead.
And then I heard shots.
There was people, maybe, tried to run away or something.
And there was a lot of screams, and shots.
And, and I kind of chickened out, and I didn't go any farther.
So from there, I walked away.
And I started to walk out of the center of the town to the suburbs.
I remember I came in to one Polack that lived-- he was-- I don't know if he was a farmer.
But he, he had like a farmhouse or something on the outskirt of the town.
I walked into him.
And I asked him if he could give me anything to drink, you know.
So he gave me some water.
I must have drink about a gallon of water.
And he seen me, that I wasn't a normal kid, just walking the street.
So he said to me, you one of the Jews that they, they were finishing off over there in there?
I said, yes.
And he kept on asking me what happened, and so on.
And I told him the whole thing.
So he gave me some food.
He cleaned me up a little bit.
And he said listen, I'm going to do you a favor, he says.
I hope you will remember good.
Don't ever admit that you are a Jewish kid.
No, not at all.
I know he was a, a kind of middle-aged man, but I have no idea.
But he sure did me a favor.
[CRYING] He said, you find yourself a story.
He said, find yourself a name.
And never tell anybody that you're a Jew.
He said--
Well, I chose Dombrowski because that was the only name I actually remember that it could go by because it was a Polish name.
It also was one of the historical Polish heroes, you know.
Dombrowski-- Henryk Dombrowski was a hero in Polish history.
So I decided I'm going to adopt this name, and I'm going to stick to it.
So I created a story, that my father was a Polish officer in the Polish Army.
And in 1939, he was taken to a prison by the Germans.
And he was an officer.
And I come from Warsaw.
And he died in a concentration camp from the-- for the officers.
My mother was sick, and she died also.
And I live with a grandmother here and there in this town.
So I made this, you know, this fictitious story.
And I stuck to it.
And it let me, for a few months, over there, in that vicinity, to get by.
With this one, and with that one, a little bit on the-- on the railroad from the city of Sieldce, I was begging for food.
I was able to sleep with some kids that were orphans over there.
And I finally came to a conclusion that I cannot survive any more.
I have to go, maybe, rent myself out as a hired hand.
I was only 12 years old at the time.
Actually, I was exactly 12 because it was already winter of '42 and getting into '43.
So that-- I, I survived, actually, six months already, almost without anybody, just living from day to day, a little bit here, a little bit there, whatever I could.
And it got me to the winter.
And I finally found myself at a farmer.
I walked into a farm.
And this guy, I told him the story.
When they heard that I was a kid, an orphan of a Polish officer, they kind of felt sorry.
But they had a lot of respect, the peasants, for Polish officers.
So when I told him the story, and I was able to cry-- I suppose [SOBBING] I had to cry.
My heart probably ached me.
So [SOBBING] at any time, you know, I would tell him a story, I shared some tears to it.
And I was able to get myself overnight, sometimes even spend a day or two with that family.
Anyway, this guy needed a, a helper.
And it seemed to him that I was a good helper.
So he got me to work on his farm.
And how long did you remain on the farm helping?
Oh, maybe a month or so in the winter.
And I got a, a skin disease over there because he never made me wash.
And I was probably too cold and too scared to get undressed or something.
I don't know.
But I remember after that-- or maybe he had the disease, the guy, and I contract it.
It was a skin disease.
So I kept on itching, you know, between my fingers.
And it, it was terrible disease.
It kind of slowly began to eat me up alive.
I was scratching day and night.
I-- more I scratched, the more it itched me.
And I didn't get any, any medication or anything like that.
And went in month after month, and I was able, already, to find a place to stay.
But when people seen that I was scratching myself and it was a contagious disease, they threw me out.
They didn't want to-- they never knew that I was a-- I was a Jew.
But they didn't want to have nothing to do with me because they were afraid that I might, you know, infect them with this skin disease.
So they kept on throwing me out.
I would come to somebody, stay for a night, a day.
And they knew that I was scratching.
And right away, they would recognize.
It was a known-- a known disease-- a known skin disease.
They called it [POLISH] in Polish.
I don't know--
--exactly how they call it in English.
It's a-- it's a rash.
It's a itchy rash.
It's a bad rash.
Anyway--
--I walked in once to a priest.
And when I walked through the-- you know, through the winter, I always looked for a richer house because people didn't have any food for themselves.
But if I walked into a richer house, there were always some more food.
And I, I played the game pretty good.
So I never walked in to a-- to a farmer that looked pretty dilapidated.
And I always walked into a better-built home.
I walked into this house.
And I would introduce myself who I am.
So they let me sleep over.
I walked in-- I seen a parish priest.
I walked into the priest.
And I spoke to him, and I told him who I am and everything.
And I explained him that I have a disease.
Not as a Jew, but I explained that I'm a Polack, and all that.
I'm Polish kid, and my father, and all this.
This never changed.
This story stuck with me all the way.
So he says to me-- I told him that I would probably be able to hire out, to get myself work and everything if I can only get through with this disease.
Because I cannot get rid of this disease.
And then, I had, prior to that, some-- what I-- it's detail that I-- a Polack took me in once.
And he tried to get me some medication.
And of course, he pulled my pants off and saw that I was a Jew and tried to sell me out or whatever.
Anyway, I got to this priest, and I told him this story.
So he said, I'll tell you, there is, within maybe 10 kilometers, a hospital that-- you go over there.
And there is a very rich man that is-- he owns a lot of land.
He's a landowner over there.
He's one of these noblemen, a Polack, and you tell him this story.
Tell him that I send you.
The only thing-- he's going to have a problem getting into the hospital because the hospital is run by Germans, and all that.
And I had no choice.
I figured, if I continue like that, I'm-- I was falling apart already.
So I got myself to this nobleman, I told him the story, and he said, let me see what I can do for you.
Walked away, and he must have talked it over with a doctor from the hospital.
Anyway, they accepted me.
They accepted me.
And they put me up in this hospital.
The hospital was ran by nuns.
Nuns were the nurses.
And he was the doctor, this German doctor.
He was a Volksdeutsche, what they call it.
He, he was from Polish origin.
But he spoke Polish well, of course, and he spoke German.
So he was the guy that took care of this.
And I laid there for about, I don't know, maybe four or five days.
He came over to me on one of his rounds, looked me over, and looked how I look and all that.
It was fine.
The nurses-- I mean, the nuns would apply a medication every day on me.
But I was able to turn away, with my back to them, and apply on my-- on my parts, you know, by myself.
So they were not too anxious to, to do that kind of work.
They were just glad that I-- they applied it on my back and all that.
But on my front, on my-- I was able to do it by myself.
So they didn't pay much attention to it.
I took care of that until this doctor came to see me again.
He came on another visit.
I was there almost two weeks in the hospital.
And he came, and he looked at me, and he says, oh, you're healing very nicely.
And I was with my pajamas.
So I took off my top, and he looked it over.
I didn't think he would go any farther.
Anyway, he said, well, let's see the other part.
And I had to pull my bottom of my pajamas off.
And I don't know, I was all scared.
And he walked away.
I don't know if that was at the end of his round or something, but I was pretty scared that he saw me and he identified me that I was a Jew.
And I thought that this would be the end of me.
Anyway, he did not recognize me.
For some reason, he did not recognize me.
He didn't expect me, probably, to be a Jewish kid.
For whatever it was, I met, over there, a farmer next to my bed.
And this farmer laid there with an infectious hand.
And he says-- it was going to spring already, 1943 spring.
And he says to me, if you want to work for us, you go to this-and-this address.
And I will be home soon.
You tell my younger brother over there that I send you.
And you'll be able to do-- you're going to be our shepherd.
And the farmers usually have a shepherd for the summer.
In the winter, they get rid of them.
And they take care of their farms by themselves.
And this was like, already, in-- at the end of March.
It was pretty close to spring.
So I came over to his brother.
And his brother was very happy to see me, that I will be working for them.
I was kind of pleasant, and all that.
And especially coming-- that his brother sent me.
I spent the night over there.
The next day, a Jewish-- a Jew showed up.
OK, we have to go to the next tape.
OK.
